title: Gaussian mean width strong converse bound on the classical identification capacity of quantum channels
abstract: We establish a single-letter and efficiently computable strong converse bound on the classical identification capacity of quantum channels. By equipping the $n$-fold channel output space with a product state-weighted $σ$-Euclidean geometry, we allow trace-distance separation constraints for identification codes to be controlled by Euclidean covering estimates. Using Sudakov's inequality, we bound the covering numbers of the $n$-fold channel outputs via their Gaussian mean widths in the weighted geometry, whose exponential growth in $n$ is governed by the operator norm of a single-letter positive operator. Upon optimizing over all weighing states $σ$, this yields a strong converse bound on the identification capacity of the channel, which also admits a semidefinite representation. Our method improves the best known converse bounds on the identification capacity of several important examples, such as depolarizing, Pauli, erasure, and amplitude damping channels. We also discuss extensions of this method to more general Euclidean geometries on the output space.
summary: This paper develops a strong converse bound for the classical identification capacity of quantum channels by utilizing the Gaussian mean width of the channel's output space. The core technique involves relating the metric complexity of the channel image to the operator norm of a specially constructed singular operator. This yields improved, general bounds for several important quantum channels.
main_problem: The paper aims to establish a single-letter and efficiently computable strong converse bound on the classical identification capacity of quantum channels.
main_result: The identification capacity is bounded by the infimum over all reference states $\sigma$ of the logarithm of the operator norm of a derived singular operator, $\inf_{\sigma} \log \|Q_{N, \sigma}\|_\infty$.
method: The method connects the metric complexity of the channel output space, quantified by the Gaussian mean width, to the identification capacity using Sudakov's inequality.
model_or_system: The analysis concerns general quantum channels $N: \mathcal{L}(A) 	o \mathcal{L}(B)$ acting on finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, relating to quantum information transmission tasks.
key_observables: Classical identification capacity ($	ext{CID}(N)$), Gaussian mean width ($w_G$), and the $\sigma$-weighted singular operator ($Q_{N, \sigma}$).
important_parameters: The noise/depolarizing/erasure probability parameter ($p$) is used when analyzing specific examples like the depolarizing or erasure channels.
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, and the method relies on relating code size bounds to Gaussian covering estimates.
figures_summary: Figures compare the derived strong converse bounds (Gaussian, Ellipsoid) against known achievability bounds for specific channels like the depolarizing ($D_p$), Pauli ($N_p$), and erasure ($E_p$) channels.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theory by first defining weighted Euclidean geometries and relating code size to Gaussian width. It then derives the strong converse bound using the Gaussian mean width, culminating in the final optimization over reference states $\sigma$ which admits an SDP formulation.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful, geometrically motivated tool (Gaussian mean width) to derive fundamental limits (converse bounds) on quantum information tasks, which is crucial for understanding the ultimate performance limits of quantum communication protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Strong converse and achievability bounds on the classical identification capacity of the qubit de- polarizing channel Dp (see Eq. (4.18)). The blue curve shows the Gaussian converse bound from Theorem 1.2 (see Eq.(4.21)). The orange curve shows the Ellipsoid converse bound from [38] (see Eq. (4.23)). The purple and red curves show the classical capacity [38] and quantum capacity [5] converse bounds (see Eqs. (4.24) and (4.26)), respectively. The dashed curve shows the achievability bound from [15, 36] (see Eq. (4.32)).

Figure 2: Strong converse and achievability bounds on the classical identification capacity of the qubit depolarizing channel Dp. The blue and orange curves show the Gaussian and Ellipsoid strong converse bounds from Eqs. (4.21) and (4.23), respectively. The purple and red curves show the classical capacity and quantum capacity strong converse bounds from Eqs. (4.24) and (4.26), respectively. The dashed curve shows the achievability bound from Eq. (4.32).

Figure 3: Strong converse and achievability bounds on the classical identification capacity of the qubit Pauli XY channel Np. The blue curve shows the Gaussian strong converse bound from Eq. (4.36). The purple and red curves show the capacity converse bounds from Eqs. (4.37) and (4.38). The dashed curve shows the achievability bound from Eq. (4.44).

Figure 4: Strong converse and achievability bounds on the classical identification capacity of the qubit erasure channel Ep. The solid blue and orange curves show the Gaussian and ellipsoid strong converse bounds from Eqs. (4.59) and (4.61), respectively. The dashed orange curve is the ellipsoid bound with σ = Ep(1/2) (Eq. (4.62)). The purple curve shows the capacity converse bound from Eq. (4.63). The green dashed curve shows the achievability bound from Eq. (4.65).

Figure 5: Strong converse and achievability bounds on the classical identification capacity of the qubit amplitude damping channel Ap. The solid blue and orange curves show the optimized Gaussian and Ellipsoid converse bounds from Eqs. (4.76) and (4.79), respectively. The dashed blue and orange curves show the σ = Ap(1/2) Gaussian and Ellipsoid converse bounds from Eqs. (4.77) and (4.80), respectively. The purple curve shows the capacity converse bound from Eq. (4.81). The green dashed curve shows the achievability bound from Eq. (4.84).
